responseSet.title: Changes in anaclitic–introjective personality dimensions, outcomes and psychoanalytic technique: a multi-case study
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Changes in anaclitic–introjective personality dimensions, outcomes and psychoanalytic technique: a multi-case study

Andrzej Werbart and David Forsström

David Forsström

Department of Psychology

David Forsström  

personality dimensions, relatedness, self-definition, psychoanalysis, outcome

This study investigates changes in latent mental structures along the anaclitic and introjective dimensions in relation to outcomes in 14 cases of publicly financed psychoanalysis. The method of prototype matching was adapted for personality assessment, and multiple outcome measures were applied. For the anaclitic cases, symptom reduction was accompanied by more mature integration of anaclitic and introjective personality dimensions, while the introjective cases showed symptom reduction without such improvement. This could indicate that sustainable change in latent mental structures is more difficult to achieve in introjective than in anaclitic patients. Further research is needed to validate these preliminary results.

responseSet.title: Lacanian psychotherapy: theory and practical
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lacanian psychotherapy: theory and practical

Steven Bambrough

Steven Bambrough

Steven Bambrough   sbambrough@tavi-port.nhs.uk

The subject of this ambitious book is the work of the French psychoanalyst Jacques Lacan (1901–1981) and the applicability of Lacanian methods in psychotherapies. The book is largely an appeal to American clinicians, to discover within Lacan's work some useful tools that may assist them in their work with patients. The reader can benefit from the author's apparent conviction of the benefits of the use of Lacan's work in the field of psychotherapy; Miller passionately supports the idea that there are distinct and valuable benefits stemming from the experience of learning some of Lacan's methods, particularly from ‘following the letter’ of the patient's speech. Within Lacanian psychoanalysis, this means having an understanding of the signifiers that make up an analysand's speech and the formal features of the words used. I will illustrate what this means a little later.

Broadly speaking, I approached this review being sympathetic to the book's aims and its subject, even though occasionally perplexed by its style and theoretical points. It is a short and highly readable book which evidently conveys the author's substantial knowledge; this is successfully combined with a humane and thoughtful analysis of the subject. The book states its aim clearly, while the use of case discussions illustrates for the clinician possible routes for an application of ‘some Lacanian principles to relatively short-term psychotherapies’ (p. xviii).

If we are to consider Miller's book a successful project, then in my view we would need to accept certain compromises:

we should leave aside for a moment the problem of defining what ‘Lacanian’ is, since this is a contentious subject,

we should momentarily suspend Lacan's own belief that psychotherapy and Lacanian psychoanalysis are incompatible with each other and

we should instead focus on whether the book achieves its aim of providing clinicians with an opportunity for familiarisation with several of Lacan's central theories.

I am impressed by Miller's grasp of the Lacanian theories he chooses to discuss in this book. Miller's teacher is the eminent Lacanian analyst and author Bruce Fink (author of some of the best books on Lacan in English, as well as translator of the first complete edition of ‘Ecrits’ in English), but I would like to present two main areas where this book may benefit from some critique.

responseSet.title: The elephant tied up with string: a clinical case study showing the importance of NHS provision of intensive, time-limited psychoanalytic psychotherapy treatments
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The elephant tied up with string: a clinical case study showing the importance of NHS provision of intensive, time-limited psychoanalytic psychotherapy treatments

Simon Shaw

Simon Shaw

Simon Shaw   *Email: simon.shaw@slam.nhs.uk
complex disorder, perversion, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, NHS provision, intensive treatment

Prof. Richard Layard's influential advocacy for greater provision of ‘evidence-based’ psychological therapies, based in part on an economic rationale, has lead to greatly increased provision of cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and short-term therapies for depression, through the Increasing Access to Psychological Therapies (IAPT) initiative, whilst longer-term psychodynamic treatments are under threat, criticised as lacking an evidence base. This paper argues for the continuing provision of intensive thrice-weekly psychoanalytic psychotherapy treatments, time-limited to two years. It does so by describing such a treatment within an NHS psychotherapy department with a patient with a long history of severe personality pathology, including significant levels of perversity. The paper describes the patient's difficulties and his movement through the treatment, focussing on his difficulties making genuine emotional contact, his destructiveness of such contact, his acting out in the treatment, his perversity and the vital working through of the ending. The shifts the patient made are described, and follow-up information on the patient is given. The paper discusses the unique benefits of time-limited treatment and intensive psychoanalytic treatments for this ‘hard-to-help’ group of patients, and the economic rationale that can be made for such treatments.

responseSet.title: Transformation: Jung's legacy and clinical work today
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transformation: Jung's legacy and clinical work today

Richard Mizen

Richard Mizen

Richard Mizen   Email: r.f.mizen@exeter.ac.uk

A friend and colleague trained in a Freudian tradition commented to me recently that like many other ‘Jungians’ that he knew, I had ‘gone over’, meaning that he detected little difference between the assumptions, clinical and theoretical, that he held compared with mine. His assumption and the comment that it gave rise to, like others over the years in a similar vein, was the occasion for mixed feelings for me. On the one hand it is gratifying to find common ground and shared perspectives; on the other hand there is disappointment that the provenance of at least some of these shared ideas is unknown or unacknowledged. In the end I suppose it does not matter very much, but as Richard Carvalho comments in Chapter 2 of this book the concept of an unrepressed unconscious is ‘only one aspect of Jung's legacy that is in danger of being assimilated seamlessly and without acknowledgement into psychoanalysis’ (p. 31).

In Britain, since the 1930s at least, a tradition has evolved of close contacts, clinical, personal and professional, between some analytical psychologists and psychoanalysts; these exchanges have continued to both link and differentiate the ideas of Freud and his successors and those of Jung and his. For some analytical psychologists, this has arisen out of a spirit of regret that the disagreements between Jung and Freud ended in acrimony and split, arguably to the detriment and impoverishment of the contribution each had to make – notwithstanding important differences between them – and something of this spirit I think permeates this book.

The book provides a cross section of the contemporary work and preoccupations of a number of analytical psychologists who are mostly, but not exclusively, members of the Society of Analytical Psychology; all chapters reflect a tradition which values many of Jung's ideas, clinical and theoretical, but has also sought to extend and develop these. Several of the authors, for example, draw extensively upon the work of Michael Fordham and his seminal contributions which undertook to test and apply Jung's model to, amongst other things, child development, matters of technique and especially the transference–countertransference relationship. Stefano Carta's final chapter on the nature of friendship addresses the relationship between Freud and Jung directly and what he considers to be their shared failure to come to terms with the tension between the need to both find common ground but also to preserve the integrity conferred by the recognition of difference. responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley  

Welcome to the final issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy for 2014, which contains three substantial papers, each highly relevant to the journal's core values in addressing the theory, research and applications of psychoanalytic psychotherapy within the public sector.

In the first paper, entitled ‘Towards a relational affective theory of personality disorder’ Susan Mizen introduces a novel and innovative model of personality disorder based on her extensive clinical experience of working in psychotherapy services in the National Health Service (NHS), initially at the Cassel Hospital in London, and subsequently in the psychoanalytic psychotherapy service she set up in Devon. Here she has adapted the Cassel's therapeutic residential and community treatment model for personality-disordered patients detained under the Mental Health Act and presenting with the most severe psychopathology, levels of co-morbidity and risk. Mizen is remarkable in having put forward a successful business case to the local commissioners which argued that her new service, offering local intensive psychotherapeutic work with patients with severe and complex personality disorders, would be cheaper and more effective than the locked placements, many of which were private and far from the patients' homes, where the patients were placed at that time. This important achievement, however, provides only the background context for this paper in which Mizen proposes an important new theory of personality disorder and in which she demonstrates an impressive ability in applying complex psychoanalytic concepts to make sense of some of the most disturbed symptoms and behaviours with which her patients present. Her theory focuses on a psychoanalytic understanding of the ‘claustro-agoraphobic’ dynamics of the defensive structure underlying these patients' apparently different and discrete diagnostic categories, which links the patient's symptomatic manifestations to their personality structure as a whole. Mizen's work is very important in demonstrating how psychoanalytic theorising can be coherently and convincingly translated into a cost-effective service model which she has successfully established within the shifting sands of England's mental health services and commissioning structures.

responseSet.title: Surviving as a personality disorder service
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Surviving as a personality disorder service

Barry Jones and Thomas Bradshaw

Thomas Bradshaw

Thomas Bradshaw  

mentalizing, unheimlich, personality disorder

Personality disorder services working along psychotherapeutic lines gained appeal with Health Care Commissioners following the publication of the National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence (NICE) guidelines for Borderline & Antisocial Personality Disorders and the emerging successful application of psychoanalytically derived approaches. In parallel to this creative development, the economic downturn has faced the National Health Service (NHS) with considerable challenges in its ongoing survival. The potential for creative pursuit often then sits in competition, juxtaposed with the need to survive. We examine the impact of this dynamic upon the therapeutic efforts of a newly established personality disorder service. We suggest that both conscious and unconscious aspects stirred up within such encounters are demanding of the need for development of a capacity within each to bear contact with unfamiliar experiences. Through attention to that development, both patient and clinician may further enliven and enrich individual and collective identities, such that the potential to survive creatively is actualized.

responseSet.title: The thinking heart: three levels of psychoanalytic therapy with disturbed children
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The thinking heart: three levels of psychoanalytic therapy with disturbed children

Arabella Kurtz

Arabella Kurtz

Arabella Kurtz   Email: ak106@leicester.ac.uk

The Thinking Heart is Anne Alvarez's second sole-authored book. It is a sequel to her moving first book, Live Company, in which she showed how a therapeutic technique that directly invited engagement from a severely autistic boy, insisting upon relatedness and meaning even if they appeared absent, offered a route to liveliness and feeling. In this second book she draws on what is now 50 years of clinical experience with very disturbed children to present a continuum with three main types of psychoanalytic intervention situated along it, each suitable for different types of patients or for different stages of therapeutic work.

The Thinking Heart is characterised by an engaging and informal writing style and a high level of integration between psychoanalytic theory and practice, in the form of sustained and detailed clinical observation. Alvarez is good at bringing disparate elements together: she applies psychoanalytic ideas to a variety of challenging and relatively unchartered clinical situations, her approach exemplifies the integration of intelligence and emotional experience communicated by the book's title (it is drawn from Donne's poem ‘The Blossom’), and she weaves both poetry and recent findings in neuroscience into her clinical discussions. However, she does tend to open up more areas for exploration than she manages to cover in any systematic way, and I sometimes found myself wanting her to narrow her focus a little in order to follow through an argument or a line of enquiry.

The use of full clinical excerpts is unusual nowadays when authors are so conscious of the need to protect patients' identity and/or to gain consent from patients or their guardians. This book is a forcible reminder of what is lost from psychoanalytic discussion when clinical material is not included, or only appears in a truncated or elliptical form. Generally Alvarez gives detailed accounts of interactions during therapy with little background information, presumably to ensure patients' anonymity. She omits to tell us whether this was indeed her aim. This would have provided reassurance that clinical material had been ethically dealt with, as well as offering her approach up as a helpful example.

responseSet.title: The internal world of the juvenile sex offender
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The internal world of the juvenile sex offender

John Woods

John Woods

John Woods   Email: jwoods@tavi-port.nhs.uk

This erudite and informative book highlights the needs of a particular group of young people who are both damaged and potentially damaging to others. The approach is highly academic, with the early chapters consisting of substantial reviews of the literature on major subject areas such as attachment theory and psychoanalytic thinking as they may be related to juvenile sexual offending. These discussions address important questions such as whether certain attachment styles are associated with sexual compulsivity and the role of early trauma in the development of the future sex offender. Relevant psychodynamic concepts such as malignant narcissism and perversion are examined in detail and considered as possible explanations for the occurrence of sexual assault. There are in these chapters many ideas and connections, backed up by research evidence, that can be useful in understanding the underlying psychopathology of sexual offending. This reader at least was frequently reminded of clinical features of certain cases, but I found myself wishing the author had included a more explicit clinical focus to the book.

A point made repeatedly is that juvenile sexual offenders are far from being a homogenous group. Clinical experience in a service such as the Portman clinic London readily shows how they differ from each other, not only in the severity of their offending behaviour but also in the meaning and significance of the offences, and therefore of the prognostic implications (Woods, 2003). For instance, there are those who may commit a sexual offence as a result of experimentation, emotional deprivation or feelings of inadequacy; individuals with these qualities need to be distinguished from those with what might be thought of as a ‘hard core’ of cynicism or callousness that precludes any concern, whether it be for the victim, or indeed the self who may have suffered harm. In this sense, there is no one ‘internal world’ of the juvenile sex offender; there are massive differences between individuals even though, ostensibly, they may have committed a similar offence. Certain patterns, however, are important to recognise, for example, that the majority of adult sex offenders began their offending in adolescence (p. 5). What is made less clear in this book is the proportion of juvenile sex offenders who go on to offend as adults. There is evidence that the more psychopathic offender will continue their sexual offending (pp. 49–51).

Mothering without a home: attachment representations and behaviours of homeless mothers and children

Gabrielle Brown

Gabrielle Brown

Gabrielle Brown   Email: gabrielle.helix@btinternet.com

This book opens with powerfully moving accounts of psychoanalytic psychotherapy with homeless children and their mothers in inner city America. It develops into a rich and scholarly work whose contribution is relatively unique – the homeless are seldom deemed suitable patients for psychotherapy and homeless mothers are particularly vulnerable to being written off as too ‘hard-to-reach’ for research and treatment. Smolen researches and describes the growth of attachment and mentalization in mothers and children who are engaged in therapy and group work. Along the way, she details the development of her own therapeutic technique and theoretical models as she takes psychoanalytically informed work outside its usual habitat.

Ann Smolen is a child, adolescent and adult psychoanalyst at the Psychoanalytic Centre of Philadelphia, USA. The first part of the book (Chapters 1–3) describes therapy in a homelessness hostel undertaken at the start of Smolen's psychoanalytic career. The next chapters (4–8) take as their basis a research project on the effects of therapeutic interventions, which Smolen conducted at the same shelter several years later. The book's six appendices detail the research tests of attachments, well-being and cognitive functioning. The data are analysed and discussed from both an attachment and psychodynamic perspective and Harvard Psychoanalyst, Alexandra Harrison provides a concluding commentary on the research findings (Chapter 9). A generous collection of references, from largely American sources, makes the book particularly interesting for the reader more familiar with UK traditions of thought on child development.

Mothering and the ‘un-housed’ mind

I work in London as a psychodynamic psychotherapist with homeless adults accommodated by a national housing charity. A psychotherapeutic view of homelessness conceives it as more than simply deprivation of suitable accommodation. Homelessness also constitutes the end point of a complex history of individual traumas and psychosocial exclusions, which exacerbate states of dislocation, mistrust and unease. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Evidence for psychodynamic psychotherapy in specific mental disorders: a systematic review

Falk Leichsenring and Susanne Klein

Susanne Klein

Clinic of Psychosomatics and Psychotherapy

Susanne Klein  

psychodynamic psychotherapy, empirically supported treatments, psychotherapy outcome research, evidence-based medicine

This article reviews the empirical evidence for psychodynamic therapy for specific mental disorders in adults. According to the results presented here, there is evidence from randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that psychodynamic therapy is efficacious in common mental disorders, including depressive disorders, anxiety disorders, somatoform disorders, personality disorders, eating disorders, complicated grief, posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD), and substance-related disorders. These results clearly contradict assertions repeatedly made by representatives of other psychotherapeutic approaches claiming that psychodynamic psychotherapy is not empirically supported. However, further research is required, both on outcome and processes of psychodynamic psychotherapy. There is a need, for example, for RCTs of psychodynamic psychotherapy of PTSD. Furthermore, research on long-term psychotherapy for specific mental disorders is required.

The ultimate taboo? An exploration of female violence and perversion

Anna Motz

Anna Motz

Anna Motz   *Email: apmotz@aol.com
perversion, abuse, idealisation, maternal violence, taboo, narcissism, forensic psychology, murder

The author explores the development of female violence and its typical expression in acts of aggression directed towards the woman's own body and those of her children. She explores the societal resistance to confronting the disturbing reality of maternal abuse in the light of commonly held, cherished beliefs about femininity in general and motherhood in particular. The hidden nature of female violence, so often enacted in the private, domestic realm, is evident in the clinical case material presented. The author presents a model of the psychology of female violence described as ‘crimes against the body’.

Psychoanalysis, the secure society and the role of relationships

Susanna Abse

Susanna Abse

Susanna Abse   *Email: sabse@tccr.org.uk
relationships, psychoanalysis, society

There is growing concern about how people respond to a fast-changing world in the context of economic decline. Symptoms of distress such as depression and anxiety are reaching epidemic proportions alongside the breakdown of family stability. This paper considers how psychoanalysis can enter the debate about these concerns and whether psychoanalytic theory and practice in the UK has concentrated too much on the individual to the exclusion of the wider family and the community.

responseSet.title: Does psychoanalytic psychotherapy offset use of mental health services and related costs in severe borderline personality disorder? – A case study
responseSet.authorMast: Maria Grazia Turri and  Lucia Andreatta
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Does psychoanalytic psychotherapy offset use of mental health services and related costs in severe borderline personality disorder? – A case study

Maria Grazia Turri and Lucia Andreatta

Lucia Andreatta

Oxford Health NHS Foundation Trust

Clinical Research Facility, Warneford Hospital

Lucia Andreatta  

psychoanalytic psychotherapy, borderline personality disorder, cost offset, case study

Aims and methods: To show that long-term psychoanalytic psychotherapy can offset costs of psychiatric care when offered to a patient affected by borderline personality disorder with a severe presentation and co-morbid psychopathologies. Costs of mental health services were calculated for a nine-year period. Cost offset was calculated comparing the five years preceding therapy with the four years during and post-therapy. Results: In this case study the provision of long-term psychoanalytic psychotherapy has consistently and progressively reduced use of mental health services and related costs during the intervention and post-intervention period. Significant reduction in use of services and savings of approximately £5000 were obtained for the post-intervention year. Limitations: The research findings are limited by the lack of control conditions over confounding factors. Besides, cost-effectiveness could not be established. Clinical implications: Long-term psychoanalytic psychotherapy is rarely available in the modern National Health Service (NHS) and is unlikely to be offered to patients with severe character pathology. Although specialist tertiary services have been created to cater for this group, patients who cannot engage with these services risk not to be adequately treated. This case study suggests that long-term psychoanalytic psychotherapy could be used as a viable and cost-saving alternative for some of these patients.

[[RunningHead]]

Learning from women with psychosis

Siobhan O'Connor

Siobhan O'Connor

Siobhan O'Connor  

psychosis, female, aggression, manic, inpatient

The article describes psychoanalytic reflections in the work of a consultant psychiatrist responsible for a recently developed female intensive care unit. The manifestations of aggression were observed to be different from that of male patients. The behavioural manifestation of aggression by women was linked to the themes of secrets, and of hidden aggression, which are described in the analytic literature as feminine characteristics. The relationships between women as sibling inpatients and with female staff may explain some of the difficulties in managing aggression. Hidden aggression in the dyad of female-to-female transferences and countertransference may contribute to problems in all female inpatient settings. Bringing together the themes of psychosis and of women from a psychoanalytic perspective, the author suggests that the care pathway leading to transfer to the intensive care unit may exacerbate psychotic symptoms in acute states, due to specific vulnerabilities of women. The provision of specialized inpatient services for women may be more therapeutic if female development and the nature of their relationships were better understood.

Further learning from the patient, the analytic space and process (2nd ed.)

Judith Trowell

Judith Trowell

Judith Trowell   Email: judith@trowellj.fsnet.co.uk

Patrick Casement is an inspiring psychoanalyst. He is highly respected as a very talented clinician, but his special gift is also the capacity to write about his work. He is able to describe what he has learnt and understood in a way that is easily grasped by others working in this field, be they analysts, psychotherapists or psychodynamic counsellors. It is in recognition of this that his books ‘Learning from Experience’ and now ‘Further Learning from the Patient’ are being reissued. ‘Further Learning from the Patient’ was first published in 1990 and is now reissued in 2014. There is a new introduction by Professor Maria Gilbert which is an accolade to his insights and to his capacity to write in a way that means his thoughts are available to those trying now to use psychoanalytic ideas.

The initial chapter ‘Beyond Dogma’ is his account of his journey to psychoanalysis from religious life, to social work and on to psychoanalysis. It places the man in his context, and helps us to understand his centredness and his sense of containment. This initial chapter also highlights his key ideas: the importance of transference and countertransference, the value of trial identification prior to interpretation, the ‘internal supervisor’ (distinct from the internalised external supervisor) and the importance of allowing himself not to know, but to follow the patient.

These themes recur and are elaborated in subsequent chapters. Whilst in the room with a patient it is often obvious what is going on, but the skill lies in knowing when and how to interpret. He explains this crucial skill for a therapist, the how and when to interpret, with telling clinical examples.

His lengthy example is very moving and instructive, a description of managing and recognising the patient's sexuality, and of responding in a way that is helpful to their understanding. This is beautifully illustrated. He goes on to use Matte Blanco's idea of unconscious sets as a way of thinking about the impact of trauma, a way of thinking which was new to me. He goes on to give clinical examples. One of a baby girl traumatised by an injection and the preceding removal of her clothes is so simple, and yet so profound. Within this chapter on trauma he talks about the ‘double transference’, the transference from the trauma in the past, together with the transference that is part of the discovery of the difference that is the analyst not the abuser. He is suggesting, I think, that the patient experiences in the transference at times that the analyst has become the abuser, but at other times realises that the analyst is attempting to be thoughtful and able to manage the distressing 411

Give sorrow words: working with a dying child

Janine Sternberg

Janine Sternberg

Janine Sternberg   Email: janines@bpf-psychotherapy.org.uk

This is a reissued edition of the book that Dorothy Judd wrote in the 1980s, which was first published in 1989. It was a seminal work then and still richly deserves to be accessible to new generations of those working with ill or dying children. As is stated in the current foreword to the book, ‘our need to prepare for, to understand, to experience death remains fundamental’. This book is written in a fluent way, with many quotations from literature that seem so apposite, reminding us that there is nothing new in human suffering, although the particular way it is experienced may be modern.

The heart of the book is Judd's account of her therapeutic meetings with 7-year-old Robert over the period of a few months following a bone marrow transplant up to his death. Her bravery in both doing this difficult work and in writing about it makes me proud to be a colleague, a fellow child and adolescent psychotherapist. Her descriptions of his physical suffering are excruciating and the way in which she introduces us to his, his family's and her pain and suffering is remarkable. She suggests (p. xvii) that the book may help us to think ‘about that which perhaps is not unthinkable’ while at the same time recognising that the death of a child is ‘an outrage’.

This is the third edition, with the second having been published in 1995. As far as I can see, little has been added to the main body of the text, while it is clear from the works of others cited that a considerable number of new studies that took place in the early 1990s were added for the second edition. What brings this edition up to date is a very substantial introduction by Claudia de Campos, who works in a hospital setting. She brings in the ‘outside world’ of policy documents as well as advances in the field since the book was first written. Her scholarly introduction brings in statements from leading medical staff in the field. These are well woven into her narrative and show, in many excerpts, great honesty and humanity. While still giving attention to the poor prognosis that certain diagnoses can have, the reader gets a heartening sense of massive improvements in treatment that have taken place over the last 25 years. The improvements in survival also lead to the need to become even more aware of the psychological sequelae of experiencing a life-threatening illness and undergoing invasive treatment. We are reminded that trauma can impair a child's capacity to symbolise. Judd points out that symptoms of anxiety and sadness are a natural response to life-threatening illness and end of life, so that pathological anxiety and clinical depression are frequently undiagnosed and therefore untreated.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The ‘blame and shame society’

Jean Knox

Jean Knox

Department of Psychology

Jean Knox   †This article is based on a talk given at the Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy NOW Conference, 2013. A version was originally published in Journal of Psychological Therapies in Primary Care (Vol. 3, No. 1, April 2014) who kindly gave permission for the publication of this subsequent version.
blame, shame, powerlessness, society, psychotherapy

In this opinion piece, I explore some of the social and cultural factors that contribute to the creation of feelings of shame in those members of society who are vulnerable or disadvantaged in various ways. I suggest that a ‘blame and shame’ attitude has become pervasive in today's political culture, reassuring the comfortable and privileged that they deserve their own success and allowing them to blame the disadvantaged for their own misfortune. Those who feel that they must become invulnerable in order to succeed therefore project their own vulnerable child onto the vulnerable in our society and attack and condemn in others what they most fear in themselves.

Perverting the course of therapy: the fetishisation of governance in public sector mental health services

Rosemary Rizq

Rosemary Rizq

Department of Psychology

Rosemary Rizq   †This paper is an expanded version of a talk given at the Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy NOW 2013 conference in London, October 2013. A ‘Fieldnote’ version of this paper has been published in Psychoanalysis, Culture & Society in June 2014 (Perversion, neoliberalism and therapy: The audit culture in mental health services, Psychoanalysis, Culture & Society, 19, 209–218; doi:10.1057/pcs.2014.15).
fetishisation, IAPT, Lacan, mental health, NHS, NPM, organisational dynamics, perversion, psychoanalysis

In this paper, I draw on Freudian and Lacanian psychoanalytic theory to consider the notion of perversion and fetishisation within the context of contemporary UK public mental health services which have been subject to New Public Management restructuring. Offering an organisational case example based on clinical experience within an expanded NHS mental health service, I explore how services that are subject to neo-liberal regulatory and performance management systems sponsor a perverse organisational solution to the anxieties and difficulties of dealing with psychologically distressed patients. I conclude that theorising the unconscious dynamics of perversion and fetishism may provide an opportunity to rethink governmentality, offering a potentially fruitful means of addressing recent political concerns about the negative impact of a ‘target culture’ on public sector health services.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Maternal lineage: identification, desire and transgenerational issues

Geraldine Shipton

Geraldine Shipton

Geraldine Shipton   Email: gashipton@tiscali.co.uk

Paola Mariotti points out in her introduction to this new book that psychoanalysis has paid much more attention to the importance of the mother–baby relationship from the perspective of the baby's mind and body than the mother's mind and body. This book is a step towards redressing the balance by gathering together 14 key texts on various aspects of maternality written almost entirely by women from a number of countries (the sole male author is Winnicott). Many of the chapters have been published elsewhere, but are given coherence as a collection by juxtaposition and by helpful introductory sections in which Mariotti suggests how we might construct a fuller picture of female sexuality, development across the life cycle, fertility, reproductive experiences and how they all interact in mothering.

She identifies three important organising ideas which cross-cut the papers: first, the origins and meaning of the wish to be a mother in the early history of the potential mother, indeed in the mother–baby interactions involving both body and mind; second, the centrality of identification with a maternal imago which connects mothers to their own mothers and is a conduit for the transmission of particular feelings (for example, shame and guilt) and third, the role of the oedipus complex in structuring the wish for a baby. All of the chapters are useful to clinicians, but when the reader navigates them with these ideas in mind they come together more powerfully, making me at least wake up to some implications that may have been in the back of my mind but certainly not so clearly at the forefront. For example, Mariotti, picking up on Rosemary Balsam's chapter, asks why is there so little written about the impact that the changing female body, especially the pregnant mother's body, has on the imagination of the child – what does it mean for the identificatory process in a girl that mother's tummy can become so swollen? I found myself thinking that in anorexia, the ‘fat’ tummy is hated and taboo but has often been linked to fear of pregnancy in the girl, and less to envy or even a reaction formation to a desire relating to mother and her changed shape. The ego is first and foremost a body-ego, as Freud told us, but a male body is different to a woman's not just genitally but in other ways, some of which become striking in pregnancy when the breasts and abdomen enlarge – the woman's body is sensationally altered and then changes back again after birth. How does this link up with the configuration of the ego and its boundaries? How does this potential flexibility influence femininity? There is much to ponder here.

Some thoughts and responses to The Francis Report

Jane Garner

Jane Garner

Jane Garner   *Email: janegarner5097@gmail.com
psychosocial relationships, thinking environment, transparency, corruption of care, competences

This response to the report by lawyer Robert Francis into the failure of National Health Service (NHS) care in Mid-Staffordshire is fairly positive about its conclusions but points out that without consideration of personal, psychosocial factors in care, on the ward, in the institution things are unlikely to improve, the report leaves much unconsidered. Francis emphasises that patient care is at the very centre of the NHS, this is obvious but so often forgotten. He comments that the unhelpful blame culture still exists. Missing from Francis’ analysis is a psychological perspective – the systemic factors he mentions are administrative not relational. This response is an attempt to redress the balance in thinking about the improvement of care for patients and of the working lives of staff.

Psychoanalytically-informed clinical supervision of staff in probation services

Heather Wood and Gabrielle Brown

Gabrielle Brown

Gabrielle Brown  

clinical supervision, countertransference, personality disorder pathway, probation, containment

At a time of considerable organisational change within Probation Services, the authors describe a new initiative to offer psychoanalytically-informed clinical supervision to frontline probation officers, linked to the development of a care pathway for offenders with personality disorders. An analysis of feedback forms from supervisors during the first months of this project highlighted the typical countertransference challenges of probation work. In subsequent months, concerns about organisational change were increasingly evident in supervision, possibly amplifying existing concerns about risk, formulation and management of countertransference responses. The complexities of offering containment and supervision to probation officers, at a time when they were anxious about the security of their organisational ‘container’, are explored.

[[RunningHead]]

Objects of desire and the mediated self: addictions, compulsions and fetishism in the technoculture arena

Alistair Sweet

Alistair Sweet

Alistair Sweet   *Email: alistairdsweet@yahoo.co.uk
technology, mediated, self, objects, decompensation, projection

This paper explores the idea that electronic computer-based media, also referred to as the new technologies, may act as receptacles for and transmitters of projected aspects of the self. These aspects of the self then enter into relations with a new and emerging range of technologically generated objects, which may critically influence aspects of the therapeutic relationship. In the virtual worlds that evolve in relation to the new technologies, obsessional thinking and compulsive behaviours may be stimulated, precisely because split off aspects of the self must be constantly monitored as stored images, interacting with signifiers in the technological chain, often felt to be under threat from a range of potentially persecutory and bizarre objects [Bion, 1962. A theory of thinking. International Journal of Psychoanalysis. 43]. It is suggested that an intra-psychic representation of the self, the mediated self, may evolve based on a blurring of the lines of demarcation between projected/introjected elements of the self and its objects. Drawing upon clinical case material, where appropriate, the author also discusses some similarities and differences between various forms of substance addiction, computer compulsions and, more broadly, the idea of the fetishistic object relation.

Understanding and treating dissociative identity disorder: a relational approach

Joanne Stubley

Joanne Stubley

Joanne Stubley   Email: jstubley@tavi-port.nhs.uk

Although erudite, highly informed and cohesive in structure, Howell's book on Dissociative Identity Disorder (DID) presented me with a particular problem that took some time and thought for me to understand. If this book were not about DID and much of the same theoretical material had been brought together with a title of say Dissociative States or even Trauma, I would feel less uneasy and less uncomfortable in recommending it. This would perhaps have allowed me not to directly address the conflicting views within psychoanalytic societies about the diagnosis of DID. Some of the case material also contributed to my unease as I tried to conceptualise how I personally understand the psychiatric diagnosis of DID (or in European terms Multiple Personality Disorder). Perhaps, it is impossible to read these kinds of cases without recognising some link to the Hollywood versions of ‘The Three faces of Eve’ or ‘Sybil’ with the mixture of disbelief and fascination that is inevitably engendered.

I was reminded of a similar personal experience when I first came to live in the UK and took up my psychiatric studies here, having begun them in Australia where a considerable American influence was present. In the early 1990s, the Americans were breaking new ground both theoretically and psychoanalytically in working with the group of patients whom they called ‘borderline’. However, my use of this concept when I first arrived in the UK was to meet with some scepticism and disquiet at the diagnosis. This seems difficult to believe now when Borderline Personality Disorder is such a well-recognised diagnostic entity and I find myself wondering whether DID may also have a similar fate over time. If this might be true then Elizabeth F. Howell's book is likely to play a role in bringing the British on board.

Elizabeth Howell, described on the back cover as a psychoanalyst and traumatologist, provides a detailed, thoughtful and coherent account of a wide range of theoretical issues that deserve to be addressed in this diagnostic setting. She divides her book into two parts, linked to her title, by first understanding and then treating DID. The first part covers trauma theory, attachment theory and research, psychoanalytic theory including an historical overview of dissociation and neurobiological explanations of trauma. It is this first part that I was particularly impressed by. There is something deeply satisfactory in the carefully considered and straightforward way in which Howell links together the diverse fields of neuroscience with attachment and relational psychoanalysis to elucidate 235

Sandra Evans   *Email: sandra.evans@eastlondon.nhs.uk
dementia, NHS, psychodynamic, older adults, memory

The 2009 National Dementia Strategy exhorted the National Health Service to raise the profile of dementia in the UK and to make the diagnosis in 75% of sufferers within a few years. While this aspiration is commendable in the sense that ideologically it is an attempt to improve things for dementia sufferers, it is likely to have other consequences which may not be so well understood or considered. Raising expectations in a vulnerable population is fine if those peoples’ lives can be improved even a little by making a diagnosis. It is, however, a lot to ask services to deliver at the same time that the country is experiencing one of the worst economic crises of our generation. This paper sets out what a pathway into dementia diagnosis might look like using a psychodynamic framework, including giving a dementia diagnosis and offering aftercare. The author considers some of the thoughts and feelings in the sufferers, carers and the staff who offer the service; the challenges posed by a diverse group and how to deliver best practice. The services described are from within community psychiatric settings: those used to delivering not only dementia diagnoses, but also managing delusions, hallucinations and mood disturbances associated with dementia. Mental health services do not exclusively provide memory clinics and the author is not extending these observations to services delivered by neurologists or physicians in medicine for the elderly.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychologically Informed Planned Environment (PIPE): a group analytic perspective

Marion Brown

Marion Brown

Marion Brown   Email: marionbrown7@btinternet.com
PIPEs, Psychologically Informed Planned Environment, group analytic, group process, psychosocial

Psychologically Informed Planned Environments (PIPEs) are an additional stage in the Offender Management Personality Disorder Pathway. The author describes the context, the impact of the work on both offenders and staff, and discusses the importance of paying close attention to the psychological process level from both a service and an organisational perspective. It is argued that the group analytic understanding of group and individual process is an important element of the psychological containment within PIPE for both offenders and staff. The group analytic focus on the here and now relational level linking to the historical context and the unconscious group and individual process provides a structure and concept for understanding the intrapersonal, interpersonal, group process, psychosocial process and the organisational context and its impact. Reference is made to the necessity of addressing the environment offender's move onto following their PIPE experience.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic diagnosis: understanding personality structure in the clinical process (2nd ed.)

Stephen Blumenthal

Stephen Blumenthal

Stephen Blumenthal   Email: sblumenthal@tavi-port.nhs.uk

This is the second edition of Nancy McWilliams' readable and accessible book which delineates her psychoanalytically informed approach to the diagnosis of personality patterns, an essential tool for the mental health professional working with personality pathology. McWilliams' is a rare achievement, offering a clear and understandable text in what is ultimately a textbook, the likes of which can oftentimes be rather dry and incomprehensible. She situates character disorder within a psychological and social context, on a continuum with normality, thereby shifting the focus of diagnosis from a stagnant set of behavioural characteristics, to a lively, interactional and developmental context which takes better account of the person as a whole. There are some parts of the book which stretch the notion of ‘psychoanalytic’ beyond the point with which many would agree, and this may relate to differing Anglo and American cultural perspectives.

The two words in the title designate the areas addressed by the text and show the conflicts and tensions the book tries to bridge. The term ‘psychoanalytic’ denotes the book as having its roots in a relational approach incorporating the unconscious and defence mechanisms, and the term ‘diagnosis’ indicates McWilliams' desire to speak to a wider audience within the mental health field. Bringing together these two worlds is a difficult and challenging task, but for the most part, McWilliams does this admirably. She achieves this by speaking a plain language, which is not only engaging and accessible, but also captures an underlying complexity.

The book will be of value to both mental health professionals who wish to deepen their understanding of personality pathology, and psychotherapists who want to improve their understanding of diagnosis. The book sits at the interface of generic mental health and psychoanalysis, drawing from and enriching both disciplines. Inevitably perhaps, the book is written more for one community than another. McWilliams' project appears to extend psychoanalytic language and concepts to a generic setting, rather than talking to a more specialised psychoanalytic audience, although no doubt the book will be of value to this group too, albeit less so.

Infant research and neuroscience at work in psychotherapy: expanding the clinical repertoire

Graham Music

Graham Music

Graham Music   gmusic@tavi-port.nhs.uk

While there has in recent years been a huge growth of interest in neuroscience research within the psychotherapy community, there remains considerable uncertainty about whether these new findings, as exciting as many of us find them, really do have serious applications to the psychotherapeutic process. Psychotherapists from a range of diverse theoretical orientations have claimed that contemporary neuroscience findings back up their particular ideas and ways of working. A few psychoanalytic thinkers have harnessed this thinking, including, for example, Green (2003), Schore (2012), and of course Fonagy, Gergely, Jurist, and Target (2004), and increasingly we see reference to these ideas in psychoanalytic papers (e.g. Emanuel, 2004; Holmes, 2013). There is also neurospsychoanalysis, including the less developmentally based work of Solms, which has been in part trying to find an empirical neuroscientific basis for many psychoanalytic ideas (Solms & Kaplan-Solms, 2001; Solms & Panksepp, 2012). However, few have written about how this research directly helps them in their work or how it might have changed their therapeutic practice.

This is just what Judith Rustin has attempted to do in this book. Rustin (who incidentally has no links with Margaret or Michael Rustin who may be better known to UK readers) comes from a stable of American psychoanalysis influenced initially by self-psychology and later by more intersubjective approaches. She has been particularly influenced by the work of Beatrice Beebe and colleagues, both the developmental parent–infant findings and also the clinical lessons Beebe and others have drawn from such findings (Beebe & Lachmann, 2002). Rustin sets out in this book to convey her own journey of discovery of the new research and how this has affected how she thinks about and practices psychoanalysis.

One of her overt aims is to describe the brain research that she finds most pertinent and which many clinicians might not have a grip on. Each chapter focusses on a specific area of research, such as about memory or the fear system, and describes these research findings in clear and accessible language. For this aspect alone, those interested but somewhat shy of neuroscience might find that the book is of great benefit. It certainly does explicate many complex ideas in easy to understand language. Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley   jyakeley@tavi-port.nhs.uk

This Special Issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy draws together papers presented at the British Psychoanalytic Council (BPC) Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy NOW conference ‘Getting to grips with society's ills: a psychoanalytic perspective’, held in London on 5 October 2013. Inspired by the work of the Joseph Rowntree Foundation (JRF), a British social policy research and development charity that funds a UK-wide research and development programme addressing the root causes of social problems, the conference sought to explore psychoanalytic perspectives on some of the key problems in today's society identified by the JRF: the rise of individualism, consumerism and a decline of community; a decline of values; drugs and alcohol; families and young people; inequality and poverty; institutions, apathy and a democratic deficit. Drawing almost 250 delegates, the conference's talks and ensuing discussions and debates successfully demonstrated how both the academic and clinical study of psychoanalysis and its applications are of direct relevance to political discourse and social policy today.

We were not able to publish papers from all of the talks and workshops in the conference, as some of them had already been published elsewhere. This was the case for that of the keynote speaker, Salman Akhtar, whose moving and personal talk on ‘The mental pain of minorities’ provided the perfect debut for the ensuing stimulating presentations and discussions on the, often hidden, disquiets and ills afflicting today's society, and I would urge readers to access the published version of Akhtar's (2014) talk in the British Journal of Psychotherapy. However, we are very fortunate in being able to publish expanded versions of talks from almost all of the parallel sessions, each of which addressed at least one of the key themes identified by the JRF and listed above.

Jean Knox sets the scene in highlighting society's ills in her paper ‘The blame and shame society’, which addresses the assault on human values in a consumer society, and the marginalisation of vulnerability, compassion and relationships. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mind–body splitting and eating disorders: a psychoanalytic perspective

Antonella Granieri and Adriano Schimmenti

Adriano Schimmenti

Department of Psychology

Department of Psychology

Adriano Schimmenti  

eating disorders, body, splitting, affect dysregulation, developmental trauma

One of the possible consequences of affect dysregulation, resulting from developmental experiences with caregivers who have not been able to attune with the child's emotional needs, is the development of a dysfunctional relationship with food and the subsequent development of eating disorders (ED). ED patients internalize a primary sadistic object with which they can only identify in an adhesive way. The resulting ego ideal is hypertrophic and its inaccessibility generates deep feelings of shame and worthlessness. For these patients, the disordered eating behaviours often represent a way of recovering their subjectiveness and provide opportunities for a relational impact. At the same time, the body becomes a sort of ‘safe haven’ in which ED patients can imagine protecting themselves from the sadistic object's intrusive attacks. Psychoanalytic work with ED patients requires an analyst who is genuinely available to deeply listen and who knows how to reach these patients and their psychic experience ‘beyond words’. The analyst should offer the emotional support necessary to face the self-destructive impulses of a frightened and helpless self.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Towards a Relational Affective Theory of personality disorder

C. Susan Mizen

C. Susan Mizen

C. Susan Mizen   Email: susanmizen@nhs.net
personality disorder, claustro-agoraphobia, neuroscience, psychoanalysis, regression

This paper introduces a Relational Affective Formulation of severe narcissistic and borderline disorders. The formulation is grounded in psychoanalytic and neuroscientific theory, clinical observation and therapeutic work with hospitalised patients. It provides a conceptual framework for psychodynamic psychiatrists, therapists and psychosocial practitioners working in teams. Paying particular attention to the central place of affect, projective and introjective processes, claustro-agoraphobic phenomena, regression and the place of both deficits and dynamic defences in the failure of symbolisation, the case is made that this framework is suited to therapeutic work with patients who are highly suicidal and complex. The application of this formulation to service design and treatment pathways is described along with a summary of the body of psychoanalytic thinking which contributed to its development. The formulation is the cornerstone of a practice guide currently under development.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bridging the gaps

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley  

I am sometimes asked about the future direction of the journal and what type of papers should be prioritised. Should we be aiming for more research articles describing empirical outcome studies of psychoanalytic psychotherapy that conform to the modern evidence-based scientific paradigm and are needed for the survival of our discipline; or should we focus on the more ‘traditional’ papers that explore psychoanalytic theories and conceptualisations in depth, their ‘evidence’ consisting of deductions from the complex and subtle exchanges and experiences between patient and therapist in the single-case study? These questions reflect, of course, a much wider debate about the nature and relevance of psychoanalytic research that extends well beyond our own Editorial Board and the Association for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in the NHS (APP) to the psychoanalytic psychotherapy community as a whole; but also demonstrate how this debate can be so often presented as a polarisation between proponents of the ‘traditionalist’ approach on the one hand, and the ‘modern empiricists’ on the other, a polarisation that effectively bypasses any thinking about the complexity of the issues under consideration.

A quick glance at previous issues of the journal on line (using modern technology which in itself provokes controversy, as discussed in the last Special Issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy on psychoanalysis and the Internet!) shows that these questions preoccupied Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy from its inception. The journal's founding editor, Michael Sinason, recognised the importance of ‘bridging the gap’ between the methodology applied in scientific research in related fields such as neurobiology or psychology and the methods of clinical research in psychoanalysis, without undermining the importance of the latter (Sinason, 1985). Ten years later, the incoming editor, Jane Milton, echoing her predecessor in the post, Robin Anderson, writes in her debut editorial:

I would echo Robin Anderson's concern, voiced in his farewell editorial, about radical and disturbing changes in the Health Service. Although I think we can be confident that psychoanalytic psychotherapy as a discipline will continue to flourish, whatever the political struggles it may encounter, my concern is that good-quality treatment will become less available to those people much in need, who are unable to pay. Many of us are also greatly concerned that the broader application of psychoanalytic ideas to work in the mental-health field, and other fields such as social work and education, will become less and less possible, as psychoanalytic psychotherapists are edged out in the private sector. I hope that PsychoanalyticPsychotherapy may continue to contribute to the growth of scientific knowledge 1

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Survival and creativity in a changing world

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley   jyakeley@tavi-port.nhs.uk

I'm pleased to introduce this summer issue of the journal containing a wealth of stimulating papers which continue to grapple with the challenges of working, and indeed surviving, within the National Health Service (NHS). Although the first paper overtly focuses on survival as one of its main themes, all of the papers are in some way about not only how psychoanalytic psychotherapy practice may survive in the modern NHS, but how a psychoanalytic perspective may enable us to survive in an ever-changing world.

Change can be experienced as alien, hostile and threatening, and risks mobilizing the use of destructive primitive defence mechanisms that operate within and between individuals and institutions. Where there is little dialogue between those who impose change and those upon whom it impacts, a fight-flight response ensues, which may tip into a sado-masochistic dynamic and perpetuate a deadlock which stifles any room for real growth and mere survival seems the only option. But change, albeit disturbing, may also have the potential of promoting growth and creativity in a genuine sense, if losses, disappointments and resentments can be acknowledged on both ‘sides’ and worked through.

Barry Jones' and Thomas Bradshaw's paper, ‘Surviving as a personality disorder service’ eloquently describes the evolution of a new service in a London Borough and the struggles to negotiate a course through the threats and gains of financial cuts and organizational re-structuring imposed within the larger mental health trust. Whilst such changes may sound all too familiar to many of us working within today's NHS, Jones and Bradshaw highlight the unfamiliar nature of change with reference to Freud's notion of the ‘Unheimlich’, introduced in his paper The Uncanny (Freud, 1919). The authors argue that to aid the development of a robust service capable of supporting the developing identities of the patients, it was necessary to first foster the ability of staff to inhabit an unfamiliar ‘unheimlich’ or ‘un homelike’ state. Such a state may contain elements of old and new in an uneasy equilibrium, exemplified in the actual model of the service, which is an adaptation of a mentalization model (new) to include therapeutic community principles (old). This moving and sometimes painful account draws on Winnicott's ideas of transitional space, use of the object and creativity (Winnicott, 1971) to illustrate how innovation and growth may emerge from instability and uncertainty. However, when communication breaks down, as eventually occurred between the staff of the service and the organisation's management, symbolic functioning gives way to more primitive and destructive processes in which survival alone becomes the primary concern.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The outcome of psychodynamic psychotherapies with individuals diagnosed with personality disorders: a systematic review

Donna Haskayne , Rachel Hirschfeld and Michael Larkin

Michael Larkin

Department of Clinical Psychology

Michael Larkin  

personality disorder, borderline personality disorder, psychodynamic therapy, mentalisation-based therapy, transference-focused therapy

This review aimed to explore the evidence base of psychodynamic therapy for personality disorder (PD). A systematic approach was applied to the literature search with the use of a clear inclusion/exclusion criteria and a quality assessment checklist. Nineteen studies investigating the use of psychodynamic therapy for PD were reviewed. Four randomised controlled trials (RCT) demonstrated superior results for psychodynamic therapy compared with another type of therapy, two RCTs found that cognitive-based therapies were superior and one RCT found no difference between therapies for PD. Another seven RCTs found positive results for psychodynamic therapies compared with a treatment as usual condition (n = 3) or another dynamic therapy (n = 4). Five non-RCTs were included with mixed findings for treating PD with psychodynamic therapy. Many of the included studies focusing on psychodynamic-based therapies with borderline personality disorder had positive outcomes, and concentrated on mentalisation-based therapy and transference-focused therapy. There were good outcomes for short- and long-term psychodynamic therapies with individuals with PD. Implications for developing services for treating PD are discussed. Research that addresses methodological issues and increases understanding of the ingredients of change in psychodynamic therapy for PD is also recommended.

responseSet.title: Donald Winnicott today
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Donald Winnicott today

Chau-Yee Lo

Chau-Yee Lo

Chau-Yee Lo   Email: clo@tavi-port.nhs.uk

Donald Winnicott Today is a collection of essays edited by Winnicott scholar and psychoanalyst Jan Abram, whose many publications include The Language of Winnicott: A Dictionary of Winnicott's Use of Words, an indispensable companion to Winnicott studies that was first published in 1996 and went into its second edition in 2007. This new book bears the title of a conference held in University College London in June 2006 and is another significant contribution to the secondary literature on Winnicott.

It falls into three main parts. The first part consists of introductory overviews of Winnicott's thinking and practice. The second offers a series of personal perspectives, which explore aspects and consequences of elements of that practice. Part 3 reviews the contributions that Winnicott made towards the end of his life. All are preceded by the author's Introduction (pp. 1–25) in which she guides the reader chapter-by-chapter by giving a concise and informative summary for each. Abram also supplies a helpful Winnicott chronology (pp. xxvi–xxx; first published in the Education Section of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, 2008, vol. 89, pp. 1189–1217), which brings into alignment his three phases of development as a theoretician: Phase 1 – The environment-individual set-up (1935–1944), Phase 2 – Transitional phenomena (1945–1960) and Phase 3 – The use of an object (1960–1971).

Each of the three parts of the volume opens with material by Winnicott himself: (1) ‘D.W.W. on D.W.W.’ (pp. 29–45), (2) ‘A personal view of the Kleinian contribution’ (pp. 159–167) and (3) ‘The use of an object in the context of Moses and Monotheism’ (pp. 293–301). These serve to orient the reader with an overview of his thinking and contribution to psychoanalysis: they also provide a background for the re-evaluation and expansion of his theoretical ideas in the other papers. Following an appendix outlining more recent directions in Winnicott studies, a glossary supplements Abram's chapter from the first part of the book, ‘The evolution of Winnicott's theoretical matrix: a brief outline’ (Chapter 3): together these supply an invaluable guide to Winnicott's at times idiosyncratic and even obscure use of words and terminology.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lucy, Major Tom, Bion and the psychotic vacuum†

Gary Winship

Gary Winship

Gary Winship   *Email: Gary.Winship@nottingham.ac.uk
psychotherapy, substance misuse, drug addiction, psychosis, repetition-compulsion, fixation

A triptych of clinical cases binds together a new frame for understanding the relationship vacuum that lies at the heart addiction to drugs. Beginning with a case anecdote about an amphetamine user who was troubled with an underlying psychotic condition, and much taken with David Bowie’s song Space Oddity, the case draws attention to the emptiness in which the client's social relations were conducted. Bion's thoughts on the challenge of linking drug addiction and psychosis are refracted in his personal copy of Rosenfeld’s (1965) book Psychotic States, where Bion made copious notes in the margins and coined the term ‘concentration for annihilation’. Some of these notes throw new light on Bion's approach to working with psychotic fixation which he derived from working with clients using drugs as well as those who were suffering psychotic states. Particular attention is given to one of Bion’s clients whose repetition-compulsion appeared to create what we might think of as a ‘psychotic vacuum’. In the final case study the life and death of Lucy Cameron is cause for reflection. Lucy’s poem ‘The Space’ draws attention to the devastating psychotic vacuum created by substance misuse.

Thinking space: promoting thinking about race, culture, and diversity in psychotherapy and beyond

Philip Archard

Philip Archard

Philip Archard   Email: lqxpjar@nottingham.ac.uk

The majority of contributions making up Thinking Space originate from presentations made at an eponymously titled monthly multi-disciplinary learning forum at the Tavistock Clinic. This forum was initiated in 2002 by the book's editor, Frank Lowe, to promote and sustain critical thinking on matters of prejudice and ‘difference’ as they pertain to psychotherapeutic activity. The contributions encompass examinations of clinical work and personal reflective accounts as well as institutional and political histories. And a wide variety of topics are addressed: amongst them, ethnicity, race, class, sexual identity and the 2011 riots. In total, the book provides an engaging sample of the diverse and valuable work coming out of the forum; albeit no coherence will be lost if the book is consulted solely for a chapter or two according to a particular line of enquiry.

To focus on a handful of them: Juliet Newbigin provides a delicate historical overview of the complexities between homosexuality and the psychoanalytic tradition, interlinking this concern with a contemplation of what has been at stake socio-politically with the evolving theorisation of gender difference in the analytic field.

Jonathan Bradley's look at the ‘racial other’ in clinical situations vividly illustrates the sensitivities required of the therapist in the fraught terrain of work with service users for whom formal mental health settings are experienced as a strange, intimidating space. Bradley's reflections derive from his specialist work as a child psychotherapist but make for relatable reading across the different professional groups involved in child and adolescent mental health by homing in on the intermeshing of practical and emotional issues that troubled parents present with and the challenges this presents to professionals striving to disentangle them.

Joanna Ryan's chapter draws upon material taken from in-depth interviews carried out with 13 experienced psychoanalytic psychotherapists. These sought the participants' views on issues of class in the consulting room, from the perspective of being a practitioner as well as being on the receiving end as patient. Ryan's study offers insight into the ways in which class-based attacks can factor in the work of therapists from working class backgrounds when working with middle-class clients and wherein it feels as though their personal worth is as much tested as their professional therapeutic proficiency. Stefano Bolognini, an eminent Italian psychoanalyst and the current President of the International Psychoanalytic Association, writes with a light touch. He avoids jargon as much as possible and cites literature only when necessary. His oeuvre extends beyond the writings of psychoanalysts to those of gastroenterologists, Zen masters, novelists, and even dog experts. His writing has a relaxed and endearingly conversational quality. However, such ‘lightness’ never sacrifices a nuanced discourse on the topic at hand, regardless of whether it pertains to the theoretical plurality in contemporary psychoanalysis, the place of Freud in a working analyst's mind, the pivotal role of preconscious mental functioning, the décor of analytic offices, thing and word presentations, courage and counterphobia, interpretative action, psychosexuality of mucous membranes, or the relationship between man and dog. In each of these realms, Bolognini links surface and depth, clinical observations and day-to-day experience, new and old psychoanalysis, and, above all, a disarmingly personal narrative with intricate theoretical infrastructure.

Allow me now to comment upon 3 out of the 12 chapters of this book in some detail. My choice is somewhat arbitrary, although heeding to Bolognini's counsel, I leave the possibility open for finding that some deeper current within my mind, as yet un-discernible, has led me to this choice. That being said, I wish to begin my piecemeal discussion of Bolognini's book by taking up its first chapter. Titled ‘Freud's objects: plurality and complexity in the internal world and in the analyst's working self’, this chapter (pp. 7–26) starts by noting that the profusion of antiquities in Freud's office reflected his ‘need of interlocutors in effigie concreta’ (pp. 8–9, italics in the original); Freud suffered from the loneliness of being a pioneer and needed the reassuring, evocative, rationalizing, and containing company of these intriguing figurines (see also Akhtar, 2009; Carpelan, 1981, in this regard). Bolognini notes that as contemporary analysts, we are not alone. However, we too need the solace of companionship in our rather lonely and quiet work. We derive such support not so much from the statues and artwork in our offices but from the internalized family of our heroes, mentors, and, our internalized view of Freud himself. Bolognini advocates for the ‘contactability and consultability of a broad internal group’ (p. 14) while also recognizing the risks of over-simplification due to an idealized clinging to such inner presences. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intersubjectivity in therapeutic interaction: a pragmatic analysis

Jean Knox and Georgia Lepper

Georgia Lepper

Department of Psychology

UK, retired

Georgia Lepper  

intersubjective processes, pragmatics, turn-taking, narrative, perspective-taking

This study draws on recent observational research on the intersubjective processes that occur in infant–caregiver interactions. It makes the case that similar methods can be used to develop an observational approach to the dynamics of intersubjectivity in the clinical process using methods from the discipline of pragmatics – the study of human interaction. Following an introduction to the research on intersubjective processes in infant–caregiver dyads and a discussion of the pragmatic approach to the study of talk-in-interaction, we present a detailed analysis of an exchange between a psychotherapist and a patient. Rupture and repair, narrative processes and perspective-taking are observed at the level of the turn-by-turn interactions between the participants. The discussion returns to clinical concerns, discussing the implications of the findings for the study of clinical theory and practice.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Developing therapeutic couple work in dementia care – the living together with dementia project

Andrew Balfour

Andrew Balfour

Andrew Balfour   *Email: abalfour@tccr.org.uk
dementia, Alzheimer's disease, relationship quality, couples psychotherapy

This paper describes an approach to working with couples where one partner has a dementia, which is currently under development at the Tavistock Centre for Couple Relationships. This draws upon video-based methods that have been used with parents and children, as well as in psychoanalytic couple psychotherapy, to develop an intervention to assist emotional contact, communication and understanding in couples living with dementia. Our approach includes the person with dementia and their partner, focussing upon the relationship between them and using shared involvement in everyday activities as a basis for enhancing emotional contact. Evidence is reviewed of the need for such an approach, which aims to foster the resilience of the couple's relationship and strengthen their capacity to manage the emotional challenges of the situation. The importance of services holding a relational view in their delivery and the economic and humanitarian consequences of a neglect of this in dementia-care settings are also discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

‘I'm beyond caring’, a response to the Francis Report: the failure of social systems in health care to adequately support nurses and nursing in the clinical care of their patients

Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans

Complex Needs Service

Marcus Evans   *Email: mevans@tavi-port.nhs.uk
nursing, Francis, defensive system, contained, management, supervision, paranoid schizoid, container

Lord Francis was commissioned to look at why the serious problems (between January 2005 and March 2009) at Mid Staffs Foundation Trust were not identified sooner and the appropriate action taken. Lord Francis was also asked to outline what lessons could be learned to enhance patient care. The report was delivered on 5 February 2013 and contained 290 recommendations. The key message was that the National Health Service needed to put the patient first and everything else should flow from that principle. Poor standards of care should not be tolerated and staff would be expected to speak out when they felt patient care was being compromised. Lord Francis also recommended that there should be one regulatory body and that the role of the Care Quality Commission was to be reviewed.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic thinking in occupational therapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic thinking in occupational therapy

Geraldine Shipton

Geraldine Shipton

Geraldine Shipton   Email: gashipton@tiscali.co.uk

Occupational therapy is, in essence, about doing and psychoanalysis is, in essence, about talking: so what happens when some concepts and practices from psychoanalysis are brought into relationship with the work of occupational therapists? Well, some of the answers are to be found in this pioneering book by four women who between them represent psychoanalytically informed occupational therapy across four very different countries: South Africa, Italy, the USA and the UK. The book aims to make links not only between psychoanalysis and occupational therapy but between the inner and outer worlds of patients, practitioners and organisations.

The book is divided into three sections: the first is entitled ‘Psychoanalytic Theory Interwoven with Occupational Therapy’ and considers the therapeutic use of the self and maps out key concepts from psychoanalysis drawing especially from Freud, Klein, Bion, Winnicott, Bowlby and Searles. It also includes a chapter tracing the history of influential ideas from the 1950s and 1960s when an American occupational therapist, Gail S. Fidler and her psychoanalyst husband, Jay W. Fidler theorised occupational therapy practice in mental health from a psychoanalytic perspective producing seminal texts for the profession. The chapter attempts to reinstate some of the thinking from this earlier period updated with more recent analytic ideas, particularly to do with object relations and the centrality of emotions in psychic life. The outstanding aspect of all the chapters is, however, not so much the outlining of key concepts and how they might be used, but the beautifully crafted clinical vignettes which bring the book to life and are woven into all of the chapters throughout the book, to my mind holding together otherwise disparate themes.

The second section ‘Psychoanalytic Occupational Therapy: A Relational Model and Illuminating Theory in Clinical Practice’ gives us an explanation of the model which unites the writers and is pivotal to the book. This is called MOVI which comes from the name ‘Modello Vivaio’ in Italian, a way of working based on the practice of occupational therapy carried out at the clinic ‘Il Vivaio’ in Milan. The authors say that MOVI is ‘currently the only occupational therapy practice model that incorporates an understanding of the unconscious in what is done and said (or not said) within the therapeutic encounter between the client, the occupational therapist and the activity’ (p. 105). responseSet.title: The embodied analyst: from Freud and Reich to relationality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The embodied analyst: from Freud and Reich to relationality

Salman Akhtar

Salman Akhtar

Philadelphia
PA
USA

Salman Akhtar   Email: salman.akhtar@jefferson.edu

Freud's declaration that ‘the ego is first and foremost a bodily ego’ (1923, p. 26) was long preceded by his interest in the psychodynamic foundations of the somatic symptoms of hysteria (Breuer & Freud, 1895) and his astute observations of patients’ non-verbal communications in the clinical situation (Freud, 1909). Wilhelm Reich developed this line of thinking with great fervor. Before his descent into paranoid grandiosity, Reich made highly significant contributions to psychoanalysis, especially in the realm of character pathology and its treatment. More than any other analyst, he investigated and worked with embodied expression of affect and fantasy. Note the following remarks.

‘Alongside the “what” of the old Freudian technique, I placed the “how.” I already knew that the “how”, i.e. the form of the behavior and communications, was far more important than what the patient told the analyst. Words can lie. The expression never lies’ (1942, p. 152).

‘ … the patient's behaviour (manner, look, language, countenance, dress, handshake, etc.) not only is vastly underestimated in terms of its analytic importance but is usually completely overlooked' (1949/1972, p. 81).

‘The patient's expressive movements involuntarily bring about an imitation in our own organism. By imitating these movements, we “sense” and understand the expression in ourselves and, consequently, in the patient’ (1949/1972, p. 362).

Reich's work inspired psychoanalytic interest in non-verbal, postural, and somatic communications during the clinical hour. It also led to therapeutic techniques that included actual involvement with patients’ bodies. Reich himself began practicing ‘vegetotherapy’ and, later, ‘orgone therapy’. Many forms of Reichian body work subsequently emerged, including Character Analytic Vegetotherapy which, at times, went in shocking directions whereby ‘patients were treated in their underwear and sometimes completely naked’ (p. 47). Among the methods that stayed close to conventional practice, despite making bodily interventions, were Biosynthesis (Boadella, 1987), Biodynamic Analysis (Boyesen, 1976), and Bioenergetic Analysis (Lowen, 1975). Since Reich had migrated to Norway (before coming to the USA) with the Nazi takeover of Austria, that country became the seat of the greatest developments in this realm.
